papers.title: The properties of energetically unbound stars in stellar clusters
papers.author_names: Ian Claydon Mark Gieles Alice Zocchi

abstract.text: Several Milky Way star clusters show a roughly flat velocity dispersion profile at large radii, which is not expected from models with a tidal cut-off energy. Possible explanations for this excess velocity include: the effects of a dark matter halo, modified gravity theories and energetically unbound stars inside of clusters. These stars are known as potential escapers (PEs) and can exist indefinitely within clusters which are on circular orbits. Through a series of N-body simulations of star cluster systems, where we vary the galactic potential, orbital eccentricity and stellar mass function, we investigate the properties of the PEs and their effects on the kinematics. We derive a prediction for the scaling of the velocity dispersion at the Jacobi surface due to PEs, as a function of cluster mass, angular velocity of the cluster orbit, and slope of the mass profile of the host galaxy. We see a tentative signal of the mass and orbital velocity dependence in kinematic data of globular clusters from literature. We also find that the fraction of PEs depends sensitively on the galactic mass profile, reaching as high as 40% in the cusp of a Navarro-Frenk-White profile and as the velocity anisotropy also depends on the slope of the galactic mass profile, we conclude that PEs provide an independent way of inferring the properties of the dark matter mass profile at the galactic radius of (globular) clusters in the Gaia era.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: unbound, escaping, or ejected stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] N-body simulations reveal that energetically unbound stars (potential escapers) in stellar clusters produce a velocity dispersion flattening at large radii due to their persistent presence within the Jacobi surface. The fraction and kinematic impact of these stars scale with cluster mass, orbital angular velocity, and the slope of the host galaxy's mass profile—particularly in cusp-like Navarro-Frenk-White halos where PE fractions reach up to 40%. This provides a novel, observable probe of dark matter halo profiles via cluster kinematics in the Gaia era, independent of dark matter or modified gravity explanations.

source_fields.title: The properties of energetically unbound stars in stellar clusters

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Conclusions: The distribution, kinematics, and dynamical influence of energetically unbound stars within the Jacobi radius of star clusters depend significantly on the slope of the galactic mass profile, with shallower profiles increasing the number of PEs, enhancing retrograde rotation, inducing velocity anisotropy, and altering velocity dispersion in ways that vary with galactic potential and cluster properties. Introduction: This paper makes the novel contribution of demonstrating that the flattening of velocity dispersion profiles in globular clusters arises from the gravitational influence of potential escapers—stars with energy above the escape threshold—that delay their escape and significantly extend cluster lifetimes in Newtonian dynamics, thereby providing a self-consistent, non-dark-matter explanation for this observed phenomenon. Description of the Simulations: The simulations use NBODY6TT to model stellar clusters in various galactic potentials with power-law mass profiles, considering different mass functions, eccentricities, and initial conditions to study the formation and evolution of planetary equilibrium (PE) structures in circular and eccentric orbits. Properties of potential escapers: This section defines and analyzes the properties of potentially escaping stars (PEs) in stellar clusters, showing that their fraction and mass increase with both galactic potential parameter $\\lambda$ and the inclusion of a mass spectrum, due to longer escape times and ongoing dynamical interactions. Velocity dispersion at the Jacobi surface: The velocity dispersion of energetically unbound stars at the Jacobi surface scales with cluster mass and other physical parameters as derived in equation (20), providing a testable prediction that differs from MOND and includes dependencies that can be used to distinguish between theoretical models through observational data.

These stars are known as potential escapers (PEs) and can exist indefinitely within clusters which are on circular orbits.
title: The properties of energetically unbound stars in stellar clusters

papers.title: New runaway O-type stars in the first Gaia Data Release
papers.author_names: Jesús Maíz Apellániz Rodolfo H. Barba Sergio Simón-Díaz Ignacio Negueruela Emilio Trigueros Páez

abstract.text: We have detected 13 new runaway-star candidates of spectral type O combining the TGAS (Tycho-Gaia Astrometric Solution) proper motions from Gaia Data Release 1 (DR1) and the sample from GOSSS (Galactic O-Star Spectroscopic Survey). We have also combined TGAS and Hipparcos proper motions to check that our technique recovers many of the previously known O-type runaways in the sample.

source_fields.venue: Proceedings of the International Astronomical Union
source_fields.journal_name: Proceedings of the International Astronomical Union
source_fields.sections_text: Methods: The paper proposes a two-dimensional proper motion analysis method that detects runaway O stars by comparing observed Galactic proper motions to a robustly fitted background model, using normalized deviations in Galactic latitude and longitude to identify outliers without requiring external distance or radial velocity data. Results: The study identifies 13 new O-type runaway star candidates using a 2-D method with a Δ cut of 3.5, confirming high detection accuracy for tangentially fast stars while missing those with dominant radial motion, and provides indirect evidence for their runaway status through proper motion corrections, cluster associations, and bow shocks. Future work: Future work aims to improve spectroscopic parallaxes, extend OB star analyses with Gaia data, derive accurate 3D velocities accounting for binarity, expand the GOSSS sample, and integrate new Gaia Data Releases to refine the study of runaway and OB stars in the solar neighborhood. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel approach by combining Gaia DR1 astrometric data with GOSSS spectroscopic observations to improve the determination of O-star kinematics and distances, solving the problem of limited and inaccurate astrometric data for Galactic O stars that hinders precise stellar population and dynamical studies.

title: New runaway O-type stars in the first Gaia Data Release
